Adults are participating in online repeated public goods experiments. In each experiment, a group of players repeatedly makes private contribution decisions. In a standard round, each player receives an endowment of coins and chooses how many coins to put into a shared group project. In some variants, the coins begin in the shared pot and players choose how many to withdraw for private use; this is strategically equivalent to choosing how much value to keep privately versus leave in the public account. Contributions to the group project are multiplied by a factor greater than 1 but less than the number of players, and the multiplied total is redistributed equally to all group members. Thus each contributed coin increases total group earnings but privately costs the contributor. The repeated game may vary in details: the endowment, multiplier, group size, number of rounds, whether players know the final round, whether the contribution choice is continuous or all-or-nothing, whether group chat is available, whether players can use costly punishment, whether players can use costly reward, and whether punishment or reward identities are visible. Participants are real online experiment subjects trying to earn money, but they may also care about fairness, reciprocity, group norms, reputation, communication, anger, confusion, or cooperation. Generate personas that cover the full support of plausible real participants in this family of public goods games, including cooperative, conditional, self-interested, low-engagement, norm-enforcing, forgiving, retaliatory, communicative, silent, strategic, and confused participants.
